questions.prompt: The coupon code must be 6-10 characters long. Which technique most directly drives the values you would test for this rule?
options: Decision table, because two conditions combine
Boundary value analysis, because it is a numeric range with edges to probe
State transition, because the cart changes state
Exploratory testing, because the rule is vague
questions.explanation: A length range has edges (5, 6, 10, 11), which is exactly what BVA targets. Decision tables are for combining conditions, not single-range edges.

questions.prompt: Your summary report recommends shipping v2.0. Which justification is defensible?
options: All cases were executed; one low-severity cosmetic bug remains open and the high-risk discount-stacking path passed
The feature felt solid when you clicked around
The deadline is tomorrow and the team needs the release
Most cases passed, though several were not run
questions.explanation: A ship call must rest on evidence: full execution, known remaining severities, and the high risks covered. Schedule pressure, gut feel, and unrun cases are not evidence.

questions.prompt: Which item in your capstone is scored by the AUTOMATED grader rather than the rubric?
options: The clarity and focus of your test plan
Whether your bug reports match the seeded defects and your cases cover the requirements
The persuasiveness of your ship/no-ship argument
Your choice of risk ranking wording
questions.explanation: Auto-grading checks objective facts: bug matches, requirement coverage, and run completeness. Plan quality and the recommendation are judged by rubric.

questions.prompt: The rule 'order must be at least $20 to apply a coupon' is best probed with which set of values?
questions.explanation: BVA on the threshold tests just below, at, and just above the boundary - the spot where off-by-one logic errors hide.

questions.prompt: In the test plan's scope section, why explicitly list what is OUT of scope (e.g. the payment gateway)?
options: To set expectations and avoid being blamed for areas you were never testing
Because every plan must mention payments
To replace the need for a risk section
questions.explanation: Stating out-of-scope items sets clear expectations about coverage so untested areas are a known, agreed gap rather than a surprise blamed on QA.
